does: This paper introduces DVMap, a framework that improves value alignment in large language models by moving beyond coarse national-level labels to fine-grained demographic constraints. The authors extract demographic archetypes from the World Values Survey, constructing a high-consensus corpus of 56,152 samples where respondents show consistent value preferences within identical demographic groups. They employ a Structured Chain-of-Thought mechanism to guide LLMs in reasoning about demographic-value correlations, and use Group Relative Policy Optimization for adaptive anchoring of model outputs. The core contribution is demonstrating that demographic-level supervision captures intra-country value heterogeneity more effectively than national labels, enabling more nuanced and pluralistic value alignment.
connects: This work extends prior research on pluralistic value alignment in LLMs, which has traditionally relied on national or regional supervision signals. It responds to limitations of coarse-grained alignment approaches by adopting insights from social science research on demographic variation in values. The paper builds on prior work in constitutional AI and value alignment that uses supervised data to steer model behavior, while incorporating demographic taxonomy from survey research. It combines recent advances in chain-of-thought prompting and group-relative optimization methods, applying these techniques to the novel problem of demographic-conditional value alignment. The use of the World Values Survey directly connects to sociological approaches for understanding value heterogeneity across populations.
enables: This framework opens directions for more sophisticated value alignment that respects within-country diversity and demographic specificity. It suggests future work in developing demographic-aware training methods that can preserve pluralistic values while avoiding stereotyping or reinforcing demographic biases. The approach could enable personalized or context-sensitive alignment where models adapt responses based on user demographics or stated value orientations. It also points toward more rigorous evaluation frameworks for testing whether LLMs genuinely capture demographic value distributions versus simply memorizing patterns. Potential applications include developing LLMs that can serve diverse communities more equitably, and methodologies for studying how demographic factors shape AI behavior and preference expression across different use cases and regions.
